clinical_history: This ECG from a middle-aged man with heart failure shows which set of findings?
diagnosis: d) The rhythm is atrial tachycardia (rate 154/min) with 2:1 AV block.  Note the partially hidden non-conducted P waves on the ST segments (e.g., leads I and aVL).  The QRS is very wide with an atypical intraventricular conduction defect (IVCD) pattern.  The rSR' type complex in lateral leads (I, aVL) is not due to a right bundle branch block, but to an atypical left ventricular conduction defect.  These unexpected rSR' complexes in lateral leads (El-Sherif sign) correlate with underlying extensive myocardial infarction (MI) and sometimes ventricular aneurysm.  (El-Sherif. Br Heart J 1970;32:440-448.)  The notching on the upstroke of the S waves in V4 with a left bundle branch block-type pattern also suggests underlying MI (Cabrera's sign).   This patient had severe cardiomyopathy secondary to coronary artery disease with extensive left ventricular wall motion abnormalities.
